steps.prompt: Drag A and B. Watch the angle and the arc.
steps.subtitle: A [central angle](teal) has its corner at the center, made by two radii — like two clock hands. The [arc](teal) is the curved piece of rim between them. The angle at O and the arc it opens onto always read the same number of degrees.

steps.prompt: Open the angle to make a 120° arc
steps.subtitle: An [arc](teal) has the same measure as its [central angle](teal). Drag the slider until the angle is about 120° — wider than a square corner (90°) — then check.

feedback.incorrect: The arc and the angle are the same number of degrees. Aim for 120° — a bit wider than a right angle.

steps.revealText: A 120° central angle opens onto a 120° arc. An arc is measured in degrees, exactly matching the central angle that cuts it off.

steps.prompt: An arc is measured in degrees
steps.body:
The measure of an [arc](teal) equals the [central angle](teal) that opens onto it. When the angle is 120°, the arc is 120° too.
This is a measure in DEGREES, not a length. A bigger circle has a longer arc in centimeters, but the very same 120° arc measure.
The whole circle is one full turn: 360°. So a half circle (a semicircle) is 180°, and a quarter circle is 90°.

steps.prompt: Why the arc matches the angle
stages: Start at the center O. Two radii reach out to A and B, and the angle they make at O is the [central angle](teal) ∠AOB.
We DEFINE the measure of [arc](teal) AB to be the size of that central angle. So the arc and the angle are the same number — here both 120°.
Swing one radius all the way around and it turns through one full circle: 360°. That makes a semicircle 180° and a quarter circle 90°.

steps.subtitle: A [minor arc](teal) is less than 180°, a [major arc](red) is more than 180°, and a semicircle is exactly 180° (half the circle). Sort each arc.
steps.doneNote: Nice — under 180° is a minor arc, over 180° is a major arc, and exactly 180° is a semicircle.

problems.prompt: The central angle ∠AOB is 70°. Find the measure of arc AB.

feedback.correct: Correct. An arc has the same measure as its central angle: 70°.
feedback.incorrect: An arc has the same measure as the central angle that cuts it off.

problems.strongHint: The arc equals the central angle, so arc AB = 70°.

problems.prompt: Arc AB measures 120°. Find the central angle ∠AOB.

feedback.correct: Correct. The central angle matches its arc: 120°.
feedback.incorrect: The central angle has the same measure as the arc it opens onto.

problems.strongHint: The central angle equals the arc, so ∠AOB = 120°.

problems.prompt: Arc AB measures 140°. Find the major arc from A to B the other way around the circle.

feedback.correct: Correct. The whole circle is 360°, so 360° − 140° = 220°.
feedback.incorrect: The two arcs together make the whole circle, 360°.

steps.subtitle: An [arc](teal) reads the same number of degrees as its [central angle](teal), and the whole circle adds to 360°.

cards.statement: A central angle of 90° cuts off a 90° arc.
cards.note: Yes — an arc has the same measure as its central angle. A 90° corner opens onto a 90° (quarter-circle) arc.

cards.statement: The arcs all the way around a full circle add up to 180°.
cards.note: No — one full turn is 360°. A semicircle is 180°; the whole circle is twice that.

cards.statement: Arc measure is given in centimeters, like a length.
cards.note: No — arc measure is in degrees. A bigger circle makes a longer arc in cm, but the same arc measure in degrees.

cards.statement: A semicircle is an arc of 180°.
cards.note: Right — a semicircle is half of 360°, so it measures 180°.

cards.statement: An arc of 150° is a minor arc.
cards.note: Yes — a minor arc is less than 180°, and 150° < 180°.

cards.statement: If the minor arc AB is 95°, the major arc the other way is 265°.
cards.note: Yes — the two arcs fill the whole circle, so 360° − 95° = 265°.

steps.doneNote: An arc matches its central angle, and minor + major fill the full 360°.
